text: In this video we report details of robotic transabdominal enucleation of a large symptomatic rectal leiomyoma in a fifty-four years old female patient. The patient was admitted in our department complaining pain in the right inguinal crural region. During proctologic examination, a submucosal round mass in the posterior right rectal region was found. Preoperative endoscopy, computed tomography scan and transanal ultrasound confirmed a submucosal mass of seven centimeters in diameter, located in the pelvis. A preoperative biopsy was non-diagnostic.
The patient was planned for a robotic enucleation of the mass in an attempt to define the histology and relieve the symptoms.

The patient is under general anesthesia in a thirty-degree Trendelenburg position with legs apart. Pneumoperitoneum is established at twelve millimeters of mercury. The surgeon inserts four robotic and two assisting trochars, using the fourth robotic port for traction with a fenestrated grasper. The assistant applies countertraction with laparoscopic instruments. The sigmoid colon is retracted, and the posterolateral peritoneum is dissected to access a safe dissection plane. The tumor, located in the right posterolateral wall of the rectum, is dissected under direct vision using monopolar scissors and fenestrated bipolar forceps.

The surgeon isolates the rectal mass by detaching its adhesions from the mesorectal tissue using sharp and blunt dissection techniques. Vascular pedicles near the mass are sectioned between clips, and the mass visibly displaces the rectum anteriorly and to the left.

The surgeon completes the enucleation of the rectal mass by applying cranial traction through a transfixed stitch, reaching the pelvic floor. The mass is excised by detaching it from the fibers of the muscular wall of the rectum.

The surgeon ensures safe margins from surrounding structures and maintains the integrity of the rectal mucosa, avoiding penetration of the pseudocapsule. A laparoscopic entrapment bag is used to retrieve the specimen. The rectal muscular defect is sutured with 3-0 absorbable stitches in a two-layer fashion, and a hydroneumatic test confirms no rectal leakage.
Following a hemostatic review, the surgeon places a drain in the pelvis to manage postoperative fluid accumulation.
